// field engineering changes

FEC & Bodges

Trace cuts, wire bridges, chip swaps. What happens when a design meets the real world.

When you're a software guy living in a hardware world,
your bodge game better be on point.

A FEC is the polite term. A bodge is the honest one. A recurring theme in this collection: repair shops making things worse, and the work that followed.

// 01

ROM Adapter Trace Cut

FEC

A small ROM adapter for 27xx and 23xx series EPROMs with A13/A14 address line breakouts. The first version got a 1mm hole drilled through a trace to sever it — the yellow annotation marks the location. The second version refined the cut position and added two U-shaped wire bodges to repurpose the board for a different chip. Wires are formed flat against the board, not loose.

Result: Both configurations worked as intended.
// 02

SapMaster Level Shifter Bypass

FEC

The new SapMaster bottom board used TXB0108PWR level shifters, which turned out to be unsuitable for UART — garbled communication between the MEGA2560 and ESP32. Rather than wait for replacement parts, the top TXB0108PWR was removed. Most pads were bridged directly. For the UART TX line, a voltage divider was built on the TSSOP-20 pads using 2.2K and 3.3K 0805 resistors and 0.3mm wire, dropping 5V MEGA TX to 3.3V for the ESP32 RX. The unmodified chip in the bottom position shows the scale.

Result: Communication restored. TXS0108E replacements ordered from DigiKey for the permanent fix.
// 03

Atlas Vista 613 Wheelchair Lift

Rescue

A used Atlas Vista 613 wheelchair lift with a non-functional Schneider SR3B261B Smart Relay controller. Schneider provided no ICSP port, so one was bodged on — fine copper wires soldered to the TQFP-64 pins of the ATMEGA128, labelled for identification. Connecting revealed the chip was locked by Schneider.

The chip was removed from its TQFP-64 footprint, replaced with an unprogrammed part, and new firmware was loaded. Damaged components identified from a previous repair were also replaced.

Result: Lift operational. Still in daily use three years later.
// 04

Air Hockey Table Communication Board

Rescue

The communication adapter board connecting an air hockey table to its scoreboard and sound system arrived snapped clean in two. The manufacturer no longer existed. A repair shop had already attempted the job and returned it in worse condition.

CA glue was used to re-align and bond the fractured substrate. Solder mask was scraped from the broken traces and each severed connection was re-bridged by hand. The scorched area visible in the bottom photo is the repair zone.

Result: Board installed and working. The table belongs to an elderly couple who use it regularly.
// 05

Broken IC Lead Under Microscope

Microscope

A breakout board chip had a leg snapped off inside the package during an attempted lift. A repair shop made the situation worse before giving up.

The chip encapsulant was ground away to expose the broken lead stub. A 0.1mm wire was soldered onto the stub and routed out to the board edge. The work was done under an Olympus SZ40 stereo microscope. The photo shows the chip body at top, QFP leads below, and the repair wire crossing the frame diagonally.

Result: Signal restored. The shop that gave up did not think it was repairable.
// 06

Nintendo 64 Lifted Traces

Rescue

A Nintendo 64 returned from a repair shop with multiple traces lifted and destroyed. Four colour-coded bodge wires — red and orange — were soldered point to point across the damaged area. Different colours allow the wires to be distinguished from each other on any future inspection. The solder joints are clean despite the damaged pad surfaces.

Result: Console back in service.

Technology meets Trees

// Eastern Ontario, Canada  ·  hank@dna.ca
